title: FlawVerifier _preview flash-loan path ignores Balancer flash loan fee causing predictably unprofitable trades
claim: The _preview function determines profitability solely by checking withdrawAmount > depositAmount, but the flash loan path in executeOnOpportunity borrows from Balancer which charges a fee (typically 0.01%). The preview never accounts for this fee, so a trade predicted as profitable may actually lose money after repaying the flash loan with fees. The NoRealizedProfit guard catches this post-execution but gas is wasted on a predictably losing trade.
impact: The FlawVerifier flash-loan path can execute trades that are mathematically guaranteed to be unprofitable due to unaccounted flash loan fees. For DEFAULT_FLASH_DEPOSIT of 1000 ETH with a 1bp Balancer fee, the minimum profit needed just to break even is ~0.1 ETH. Any _preview prediction below this threshold guarantees a loss. The post-execution NoRealizedProfit check reverts the transaction, so no funds are lost, but the caller pays gas for a doomed transaction.
paths: 1. Pool must be stale (timeDelta > 0 with accrued interest or excess WETH in contract)
2. Attacker calls executeOnOpportunity()
3. _findViableDeposit(wealthBefore) returns 0 (direct path not viable)
4. _findViableDeposit(DEFAULT_FLASH_DEPOSIT=1000 ETH) finds a candidate where _preview predicts withdrawAmount > depositAmount but netProfit < 0.10001 ETH (the flash loan fee on 1000.1 ETH borrowed)
5. Balancer flash loan executes, _executePathStrictExploit performs deposit+sync+withdraw
6. After repaying flash loan + fee, WETH balance <= wealthBefore
7. NoRealizedProfit reverts, gas is wasted

title: _callOptionalReturn reverts on tokens returning non-bool transfer/transferFrom data breaking pool composability with non-standard tokens
claim: The _callOptionalReturn function uses abi.decode(returndata, (bool)) when returndata.length > 0. If a token's transfer or transferFrom returns any non-bool data (e.g. bytes32, uint256, or any non-ABI-bool type), abi.decode will revert, permanently bricking all operations involving that token in the WiseLending protocol. While most ERC20 tokens return either bool or nothing, non-standard tokens that return other types would be completely unusable.
impact: Any pool created with a token whose transfer/transferFrom returns non-bool data becomes permanently frozen - deposits, withdrawals, borrows, repayments, and liquidations all revert during the _safeTransfer or _safeTransferFrom call. This is stricter than the known F-003 (which only notes ignored false returns) because it causes unconditional reverts rather than silent failures. The pool's funds would be locked with no recovery mechanism.
paths: 1. Master creates a pool for a non-standard token T whose transfer() returns e.g. a uint256 amount or bytes32 status instead of bool
2. User calls depositExactAmount(nftId, T_address, amount)
3. _handleDeposit succeeds (state updated), _safeTransferFrom calls _callOptionalReturn
4. Token T's transferFrom executes successfully on-chain but returns non-bool data
5. returndata.length > 0, so abi.decode(returndata, (bool)) attempts to decode non-bool data as bool
6. abi.decode reverts, entire deposit transaction reverts
7. All other functions (_safeTransfer in withdraw, borrow, liquidation) also revert for token T
8. Pool is permanently frozen; any locked funds are unrecoverable

title: Incomplete position token data cleanup in _withdrawOrAllocateSharesLiquidation shareDifference path leaves stale entries in positionLendTokenData
claim: When _withdrawOrAllocateSharesLiquidation takes the shareDifference path (withdrawAmount > totalPoolToken), it calls _coreWithdrawBare which internally calls _removeEmptyLendingData. At that point the liquidated user's shares equal shareDifference > 0, so cleanup is skipped. The function then calls _decreaseLendingShares reducing shares to 0, but _removeEmptyLendingData is never called again. This leaves the token permanently in the liquidated user's positionLendTokenData array even though they hold 0 shares and 0 pure collateral for that token.
impact: The liquidated user's positionLendTokenData retains stale entries, causing unnecessary _preparePool calls on that token whenever the user interacts with the protocol in the future. This wastes gas and could interfere with position enumeration. Over time, liquidated positions accumulate garbage entries that cannot be removed through normal protocol operations. Distinct from F-011 which covers the same class of issue in _withdrawPureCollateralLiquidation.
paths: 1. A position holds lending shares in token T as collateral
2. A liquidator calls liquidatePartiallyFromTokens or coreLiquidationIsolationPools with receive token T
3. _calculateReceiveAmount -> _withdrawOrAllocateSharesLiquidation is called
4. The cashoutAmount exceeds totalPoolToken (pool is borrowed heavily)
5. _coreWithdrawBare withdraws totalPoolToken, calls _removeEmptyLendingData which finds shares = shareDifference > 0, skips cleanup
6. _decreaseLendingShares reduces shares to 0, but _removeEmptyLendingData is not called again
7. positionLendTokenData[nftId] still contains token T even though userLendingData[nftId][T].shares == 0 and pureCollateralAmount[nftId][T] == 0
8. Future operations on this position will unnecessarily call _preparePool(T) and iterate over the stale entry
